author: Fardeen Irani
stakeholder_type: Individual Retail Investor / Market Structure Commentator
tone: Analytical, technically sophisticated, cautiously supportive of deregulation with strong caveats, reform-oriented
executive_summary: Fardeen Irani submits a technically detailed comment letter responding to the SEC's proposal to rescind Rule 611 (trade-through prohibition) and Rule 610(e) (locked/crossed market restrictions). While broadly supportive of moving toward a disclosure-based, free-market routing architecture, Irani argues that deregulation alone is insufficient given empirical evidence that 46–93% of internalized retail orders bypass superior midpoint liquidity available on public exchanges and ATSs. The letter calls for aggressive Best Execution enforcement, mandatory midpoint-cross checks prior to internalization, modernization of Rule 605 price impact windows to one minute, dynamic Smart Order Router requirements, PFOF reform, and full implementation of variable tick sizes under Rule 612 to restore competitive parity between exchanges and wholesalers.
market_structure_topics: Retail order internalization and wholesaler economics
Rule 612 variable tick sizes and sub-penny quoting
Alternative Trading Systems (ATS) and midpoint liquidity
Odd-lot quote integration and NBBO calculation
Adverse selection and price impact measurement

positions.order_competition_rule: Implicitly supportive of competition-enhancing reforms; argues that without Rule 611, brokers must be mandated to actively scan all venues for superior midpoint liquidity before routing to wholesalers, effectively advocating for a best-execution-driven competition framework as a substitute for prescriptive trade-through protection
positions.best_execution: Strongly supportive of aggressive enforcement; central thesis of the letter is that rescinding Rule 611 must be paired with fierce enforcement of FINRA Rule 5310 Best Execution, including mandatory venue sampling, proof of midpoint liquidity checks, and penalties for blind routing to captive wholesalers
positions.tick_size_reform: Supportive; advocates full implementation of Rule 612 variable sub-penny tick sizes to eliminate the competitive asymmetry that allowed off-exchange wholesalers to quote in sub-pennies while exchanges were restricted to penny increments, thereby drawing retail order flow back to transparent lit venues
positions.access_fee_reform: Not directly addressed; implicitly supportive of structural reforms that reduce off-exchange advantages, but access fee caps under Rule 610(c) are not explicitly analyzed
positions.transparency: Strongly supportive; calls for permanent shortening of Rule 605 price impact measurement window from five minutes to one minute to ensure near-instantaneous adverse selection disclosure, enabling retail brokers to identify and terminate underperforming wholesalers

economic_arguments: Between 46% and 73% of retail marketable order shares are internalized by wholesalers at prices worse than the NBBO midpoint, representing a systemic transfer of value away from retail investors
Wholesalers provide midpoint-or-better executions for only approximately 46% of orders executed in a principal capacity, indicating structural underperformance relative to available market prices
PFOF creates a misaligned economic incentive structure where retail brokerages maximize routing revenue rather than execution quality, enabling sub-optimal internalization at scale

The inverted-spread arbitrage mechanism post-Rule 610(e) rescission will naturally attract HFT competition that collapses inefficient spreads without regulatory intervention, representing a self-correcting market efficiency argument

Full implementation of Rule 612 sub-penny tick sizes removes the cost asymmetry that historically caused retail order flow to migrate off-exchange, potentially restoring exchange competitiveness on economic grounds
liquidity_arguments: Between 40% and 93% of retail shares internalized at sub-midpoint prices had superior midpoint liquidity simultaneously available on public exchanges or ATSs, demonstrating that internalization bypasses rather than provides best available liquidity

For stocks priced above $30, between 60% and 93% of principal-capacity wholesaler executions had unexecuted superior midpoint liquidity on transparent venues, indicating the problem is most acute in higher-priced, more liquid securities

Odd-lot and sub-penny quotations under modernized Rules 605 and 612 create a highly dynamic NBBO midpoint that shifts with each new quote, requiring SORs to continuously recalibrate to capture micro-penny liquidity improvements
Midpoint-pegged retail order types on exchanges could automatically re-price to capture shifting midpoint values in real time, improving liquidity access without human intervention
Locked markets under a post-Rule 611 regime collapse the spread to zero, making execution price and midpoint identical and theoretically maximizing liquidity efficiency at those moments
competition_arguments: Rescinding Rule 611 removes artificial barriers between protected round lots and unprotected odd lots, leveling the competitive playing field across venue types
The historical sub-penny quoting asymmetry between exchanges and wholesalers constituted a structural competitive disadvantage for lit venues that Rule 612 reforms can correct

Mandating pre-internalization pings to public exchanges and ATSs would force wholesalers to compete directly against displayed midpoint liquidity rather than operating in a captive PFOF-driven routing environment
Phasing out or heavily restricting PFOF would shift broker competition from routing revenue maximization to execution quality, creating a more genuinely competitive market for retail order flow
Dynamic SOR requirements that split orders across multiple venues would reduce wholesaler market power derived from exclusive PFOF agreements and increase intermarket competition
retail_investor_arguments: Retail investors are systematically harmed when up to 93% of their orders are internalized despite superior prices being openly displayed on public venues, representing a direct and quantifiable cost to individual investors

The transition to a disclosure-based regime under rescinded Rule 611 places the burden of protection on Best Execution enforcement rather than prescriptive routing rules, requiring regulators to be more vigilant on behalf of retail participants who cannot monitor execution quality themselves

Shortening the Rule 605 price impact window to one minute empowers retail brokers to identify underperforming wholesalers and switch routing relationships, indirectly protecting retail investors through market discipline
Midpoint-pegged retail order types would give individual investors automated access to the best available price without requiring them to understand complex market microstructure

ISO-based routing technology that sweeps superior displayed retail odd lots across fragmented venues would prevent wholesalers from internalizing retail blocks at inferior prices when better prices exist across the tape
recommended_actions: Aggressively enforce FINRA Rule 5310 Best Execution by penalizing brokers who blindly route to captive wholesalers without demonstrating regular sampling of venues displaying superior midpoint liquidity

Mandate a 'Midpoint Cross' priority requirement obligating wholesalers to systematically ping public exchanges and ATSs to confirm no superior passive midpoint liquidity is being bypassed before internalizing any retail order in a principal capacity
Permanently shorten the Rule 605 price impact measurement window from five minutes to one minute to reflect modern trading speeds and force accurate near-instantaneous adverse selection disclosure

Require retail brokerages to upgrade Smart Order Routers to dynamically split and route orders to exchanges when those venues display active odd-lot or round-lot midpoint liquidity rather than routing 100% of flow to a single PFOF wholesaler
Direct exchanges to introduce midpoint-pegged retail order types that automatically re-price to the shifting NBBO midpoint in real time
Enable brokers to utilize Intermarket Sweep Orders to simultaneously clear superior displayed retail odd lots across fragmented venues rather than allowing block internalization at inferior prices
Fully implement Rule 612 variable sub-penny tick sizes to restore competitive parity between public exchanges and off-exchange wholesalers
Phase out or heavily restrict Payment for Order Flow to realign broker incentives toward execution quality rather than routing revenue maximization
key_quotes: The Commission must carefully evaluate how the removal of trade-through protections interacts with the systemic internalization of retail order flow.
Between 46% and 73% of retail marketable order shares are currently internalized by wholesalers at a price worse than the NBBO midpoint.

Between 40% and 93% of the shares in marketable retail orders that wholesalers internalize at prices less favorable than the NBBO midpoint actually had midpoint liquidity available at a better price on a public exchange or an Alternative Trading System.
For stocks priced higher than $30, between 60% and 93% of these principal-capacity wholesaler executions had unexecuted, superior midpoint liquidity sitting readily available on transparent venues.
Because up to 93% of internalized retail orders are bypassed despite superior midpoint liquidity sitting on exchanges, the SEC cannot rely on deregulation alone.

The fiduciary mandate of Best Execution must be fiercely enforced to ensure that retail investors are not continually internalized at inferior prices when superior liquidity is openly displayed on the tape.
Without Rule 611 forcing a trade to route to the best price, regulators must aggressively penalize brokers who blindly route to a captive wholesaler.
The economic incentive driving sub-optimal internalization is PFOF—wholesalers paying brokers for exclusive access to 'uninformed' retail trades.

Rescinding Rules 611 and 610(e) will successfully strip away decades of technical complexity, remove artificial barriers between 'protected round lots' and 'unprotected odd lots,' and eliminate the operational burdens of preventing locked or crossed markets.

executive_summary: Jared Albert, an individual retail investor who actively places hidden and odd-lot resting limit orders on primary listing exchanges, submits a collection of emails to multiple SEC Chairs opposing Payment for Order Flow (PFOF) and off-exchange internalization. His core argument is that FINRA-reported trades routinely trade through his resting hidden and odd-lot limit orders on lit exchanges without first checking for available liquidity, constituting what he characterizes as theft and fraud. He calls on the SEC to ban PFOF unless orders first interact with the primary listing exchange, to enforce FINRA Rule 5310 on best execution, and ultimately to ban all unexposed off-exchange trading in alignment with G7/OECD peer nations.

author: Fardeen Irani
tone: Analytical, technically sophisticated, cautiously supportive of deregulation with strong conditional caveats
executive_summary: Fardeen Irani submits a technically detailed comment letter responding to the SEC's proposal to rescind Rule 611 (trade-through prohibition) and Rule 610(e) (locked/crossed quotation restrictions). While broadly supportive of moving toward a disclosure-based, free-market routing architecture, Irani argues that deregulation alone is insufficient given empirical data showing that 46-93% of internalized retail orders bypass superior midpoint liquidity available on public exchanges and ATSs. The letter calls for aggressive Best Execution enforcement, mandatory midpoint-cross checks prior to internalization, modernization of Rule 605 price impact windows to one minute, dynamic Smart Order Router requirements, PFOF reform, and full implementation of variable tick sizes under Rule 612 to restore competitive parity between exchanges and wholesalers.
market_structure_topics: Adverse selection and price impact measurement

positions.order_competition_rule: Conditionally supportive of rescinding Rule 611 and 610(e) as a simplification of market structure, but insists that without trade-through protections, a strict mandatory pre-internalization midpoint-cross check must replace the routing mandate to prevent retail investors from being systematically executed at inferior prices when superior liquidity is displayed on public venues.
positions.best_execution: Strongly supportive of aggressive, heightened enforcement of Best Execution obligations. Argues FINRA Rule 5310 must be fiercely enforced with meaningful penalties for brokers who blindly route to captive wholesalers. Brokers must affirmatively demonstrate regular sampling and routing to venues displaying superior midpoint liquidity.
positions.tick_size_reform: Strongly supportive of full implementation of Rule 612 variable sub-penny tick sizes to restore competitive parity between public exchanges and off-exchange wholesalers, arguing that exchanges lost order flow because they were legally prohibited from quoting in sub-pennies while wholesalers could trade at any fraction of a cent.
positions.access_fee_reform: Not directly addressed, though implicitly supportive of structural reforms that draw order flow back to transparent public venues.
positions.transparency: Strongly supportive of enhanced transparency through modernized Rule 605 reporting. Specifically advocates for permanently shortening the price impact measurement window from five minutes to one minute to reflect modern trading speeds and force accurate near-instantaneous adverse selection disclosure by wholesalers.

economic_arguments: Between 46% and 73% of retail marketable order shares are internalized by wholesalers at prices worse than the NBBO midpoint, representing a systematic transfer of value away from retail investors.
Wholesalers provide midpoint-or-better executions for only approximately 46% of customer orders executed in a principal capacity, indicating widespread sub-optimal execution.

Between 40% and 93% of internalized retail shares that received sub-midpoint prices had superior midpoint liquidity available on public exchanges or ATSs at the time of execution, demonstrating that inferior executions are not a function of liquidity unavailability.
For stocks priced above $30, between 60% and 93% of principal-capacity wholesaler executions bypassed unexecuted superior midpoint liquidity on transparent venues.
PFOF creates a misaligned economic incentive structure where brokers maximize routing revenue rather than execution quality, systematically disadvantaging retail investors.

The inverted-spread arbitrage mechanism following Rule 610(e) rescission will naturally attract HFT competition that collapses inefficient spreads back to efficient midpoints without regulatory intervention.

Dynamic NBBO midpoint calculations under modernized Rules 605 and 612 create mathematical asymmetries where small odd-lot quotations shift effective spread calculations differently for buy versus sell orders.
liquidity_arguments: Superior midpoint liquidity is actively displayed on public exchanges and ATSs but is being systematically bypassed by wholesalers internalizing retail orders at inferior prices.
Rescinding Rule 611 will eliminate the artificial distinction between protected round lots and unprotected odd lots, potentially improving displayed liquidity utilization.
Locked markets under a post-Rule 611 regime collapse spreads to zero, making execution price and NBBO midpoint identical, which benefits retail investors if routing is properly managed.
Crossed markets will create temporary negative spreads that HFT arbitrage will naturally resolve, restoring efficient midpoint pricing.
Midpoint-pegged retail order types on exchanges would allow automated re-pricing to capture micro-penny optimizations as the NBBO midpoint shifts dynamically.

Intermarket Sweep Orders (ISOs) can be used to simultaneously clear superior displayed retail odd lots across fragmented venues rather than allowing block internalization at inferior wholesale prices.
Full implementation of variable tick sizes under Rule 612 would allow exchanges to compete directly with wholesalers on sub-penny pricing, drawing retail order flow back to transparent lit books.
competition_arguments: The current PFOF-driven internalization model gives wholesalers exclusive access to retail order flow, insulating them from competitive pressure to improve execution quality.

Exchanges historically lost retail order flow because they were legally prohibited from quoting in sub-pennies while wholesalers faced no such restriction, creating a structurally unlevel playing field.
Full Rule 612 implementation restores competitive parity between exchanges and wholesalers, allowing public venues to compete directly on sub-penny pricing.
Phasing out or heavily restricting PFOF would force retail brokerages to compete strictly on execution quality rather than secondary routing revenue.
A mandatory pre-internalization midpoint-cross check requirement would force wholesalers to compete with displayed exchange and ATS liquidity before capturing retail order flow.
Dynamic SOR requirements would break the exclusive routing relationships between brokers and captive wholesalers, introducing genuine competition for retail order flow.
Post-Rule 610(e) rescission, HFT firms will naturally arbitrage inverted spreads, introducing competitive price discovery pressure that replaces the regulatory mandate of Rule 611.
retail_investor_arguments: Retail investors are systematically harmed by internalization at sub-midpoint prices when superior liquidity is openly available on public venues, representing a quantifiable and ongoing transfer of value.
The Commission cannot rely on deregulation alone to protect retail investors given empirical evidence that up to 93% of internalized retail orders bypass superior displayed midpoint liquidity.
Enhanced Best Execution enforcement is the primary safeguard for retail investors in a post-Rule 611 environment and must be aggressively pursued with meaningful penalties.
Modernizing the Rule 605 price impact window to one minute empowers retail brokers to identify and fire underperforming wholesalers, creating market discipline that protects retail investors.
Midpoint-pegged retail order types on exchanges would give retail investors automated access to the best available price without requiring human intervention or sophisticated order routing knowledge.
Eliminating the protected round lot versus unprotected odd lot distinction removes a structural barrier that has historically disadvantaged retail investors who frequently trade in odd-lot sizes.
PFOF reform is essential to align broker incentives with retail investor interests rather than with wholesaler revenue maximization.
recommended_actions: Aggressively enforce FINRA Rule 5310 Best Execution with meaningful penalties for brokers who blindly route to captive wholesalers without demonstrating regular sampling of superior midpoint liquidity across venues.

Mandate a pre-internalization midpoint-cross check requiring wholesalers to systematically ping public exchanges and ATSs to confirm no superior passive midpoint liquidity is being bypassed before internalizing in a principal capacity.
Permanently shorten the Rule 605 price impact measurement window from five minutes to one minute to reflect modern trading speeds and force accurate near-instantaneous adverse selection disclosure.

Require retail brokerages to upgrade Smart Order Routers to dynamically split and route orders to exchanges when those venues display active odd-lot or round-lot midpoint liquidity rather than routing 100% of flow to a single wholesaler.
Direct exchanges to introduce midpoint-pegged retail order types that automatically re-price to the shifting NBBO midpoint to capture micro-penny optimizations without human intervention.
Enable broader use of Intermarket Sweep Orders (ISOs) by retail brokers to simultaneously clear superior displayed retail odd lots across fragmented venues.
Fully implement Rule 612 variable sub-penny tick sizes to restore competitive parity between public exchanges and off-exchange wholesalers.
Phase out or heavily restrict Payment for Order Flow to force retail brokerages to compete on execution quality rather than routing revenue.

Proceed with rescinding Rules 611 and 610(e) to eliminate technical complexity, remove artificial barriers between protected and unprotected quotations, and reduce operational burdens, but only in conjunction with the above protective measures.
key_quotes: The empirical data cited in related market structure reviews underscores severe inefficiencies in the current retail ecosystem that must be addressed concurrently through a heightened, robust enforcement of the broker's fiduciary duty of Best Execution.
Between 46% and 73% of retail marketable order shares are currently internalized by wholesalers at a price worse than the NBBO midpoint.

Between 40% and 93% of the shares in marketable retail orders that wholesalers internalize at prices less favorable than the NBBO midpoint actually had midpoint liquidity available at a better price on a public exchange or an Alternative Trading System (ATS).
For stocks priced higher than $30, between 60% and 93% of these principal-capacity wholesaler executions had unexecuted, superior midpoint liquidity sitting readily available on transparent venues.
Because up to 93% of internalized retail orders are bypassed despite superior midpoint liquidity sitting on exchanges, the SEC cannot rely on deregulation alone.
If the trade-through rule is eliminated, the Commission must strictly mandate that retail brokers actively scan all public and private venues to capture these available midpoint prices.

The fiduciary mandate of Best Execution must be fiercely enforced to ensure that retail investors are not continually internalized at inferior prices when superior liquidity is openly displayed on the tape.
Without Rule 611 forcing a trade to route to the best price, regulators must aggressively penalize brokers who blindly route to a captive wholesaler.
Public exchanges historically lost order flow because they were legally blocked from quoting in sub-pennies, while off-exchange wholesalers could trade at any fraction of a cent.
The economic incentive driving sub-optimal internalization is PFOF—wholesalers paying brokers for exclusive access to uninformed retail trades.

author: Bhavith S
tone: concerned, assertive, protective of retail interests
executive_summary: Retail investor Bhavith S strongly opposes rescinding Rule 611 (Order Protection Rule) under Regulation NMS, arguing that removing the Trade-Through Rule will expose retail investors to worse execution quality by enabling zero-commission brokerages to prioritize Payment for Order Flow revenue over best execution. The author draws on personal experience with execution discrepancies and analogizes the risk to unregulated cryptocurrency markets.

positions.order_competition_rule: Not directly addressed
positions.best_execution: Opposes reliance on subjective best execution standard as a replacement for Rule 611; views it as insufficient protection for retail investors compared to a hard mechanical rule
positions.transparency: Implicitly supports price transparency through retention of NBBO-based trade-through protections

economic_arguments: Zero-commission brokerages have a financial incentive to route orders to venues that pay the highest PFOF rather than venues offering the best price for customers
Removing Rule 611 would allow brokerages to prioritize their own revenue (kickbacks) over customer execution quality
A subjective best execution standard is economically insufficient to replace a hard mathematical rule guaranteeing NBBO execution
Retail investors already experience execution discrepancies under current rules; rescission would worsen this
liquidity_arguments: No explicit liquidity arguments made; author implicitly assumes that NBBO-displayed prices represent the best available liquidity for retail investors
competition_arguments: Off-exchange wholesalers and alternative venues compete for order flow through PFOF arrangements rather than on price quality
Removing Rule 611 would tilt competitive dynamics further in favor of intermediaries over retail investors
Conflict of interest at zero-commission brokerages distorts competitive order routing away from best-price venues
retail_investor_arguments: Retail investors already experience price slippage and execution discrepancies even with Rule 611 in place
Cryptocurrency markets serve as a cautionary analogy: unregulated markets with no trade-through protection result in chronic slippage for everyday traders
Rule 611 is characterized as the primary safeguard preventing retail orders from being traded-through for the benefit of corporate middlemen
Retail investors cannot rely on broker discretion or subjective standards to protect their execution interests given inherent conflicts of interest
recommended_actions: Retain Rule 611 (Order Protection Rule) in its current form
Do not substitute the hard Trade-Through Rule with a subjective best execution standard
Protect retail investors from order routing practices that prioritize broker revenue over customer price improvement
key_quotes: I am deeply concerned that repealing Rule 611 will bring that same lack of strict price protection to the U.S. stock market, exacerbating the problem for everyday traders.

If the strict mandate to route orders to the venue displaying the National Best Bid and Offer (NBBO) is removed, these brokerages will be highly incentivized to route retail orders to favored off-exchange wholesalers and alternative venues that pay them the most in Payment for Order Flow (PFOF).
Without the rigid mechanical protection of the Trade-Through Rule, brokerages will prioritize their own kickbacks and revenue over securing the absolute best execution price for their customers.
The subjective 'best execution' standard is not a sufficient replacement for a hard, mathematical rule that guarantees retail investors get the best publicly displayed price.
Please do not remove the very safeguard that ensures retail trades are not traded-through for the benefit of corporate middlemen.
